we are alive and today you're going to discover the ultimate and I do mean ultimate step-by-step system for closing more sales and making more money. My name is Dave Dee and with me I have marketing legend and founder of GKIC, Mr. Dan Kennedy and the fashion plate of GKIC, sales manager extraordinaire Nick Louisi and Dan Kennedy is the author of over 24 books of sales and marketing and has risen to the pinnacle of three professions. Speaking, copywriting where he's the highest paid direct response copywriter living today as opposed to a dead copywriter who's still writing copy. There are higher paid dead copywriters. And direct marketing consulting. Nick Louisi is a lifetime lifelong sales person and is a serial entrepreneur. He's risen to the top of his profession in multiple industries. He's a multiple-time president club winner. What is a president club winner? You get to go on a trip. It's exciting. Okay, excellent. And has managed multiple sales forces including the formidable GKIC sales force. During the show you're going to have the opportunity to ask both Dan and Nick your questions and so let's get started with part one which is foundations of sales success. So Dan let me ask you how exactly does what we're about to talk about sales dovetail perfectly with GKIC marketing that we teach which most people know us for? Because marketing rarely can finish the job. It can set up the job. So CNBC reported yesterday that 94% of all commerce in the United States in 2013 occurred offline. So 6% is a pretty small sandbox. And then if you wanted to take out pure entertainment from Netflix to porn and then you would pretty much have Amazon and maybe a point or two for everybody else. Right. And even that's deceptive because some of what gets counted as e-commerce actually had a salesperson involved at some place at that. You know, not to be fair that media drives a lot of offline commerce as well. But regardless of how you want to fool with that number the overwhelming majority of sales requires sales. It can't be done just with marketing and media. And it is often the place where everything we do at GKIC, the four GKIC members sort of comes to a screeching halt and collapses into dysfunction. So I have two clients right now who by their own admission now have infinitely better marketing driving infinitely better and more leads into a horribly dysfunctional end of game sales place with horribly dysfunctional inept sales people. And so all we've really accomplished at this point is exposed how bad that problem is. And oftentimes marketing gets blamed as well. Right. Well, we didn't make any money from this marketing that we sent out. Advertising and marketing almost always bears the blame because the fix for that gets delegated. The fix for the sales function often has to be done by the owner who often is part of the problem even to the point that the owner could be one of the salespeople and be inept dysfunction, etc. So the first place all the blame goes is advertising, right, which generally that discussion is that advertising brings us bad prospects. That advertising brings us badly. Then they'll blame the marketing, right, which that is that whole process really doesn't work, right, because it had at the end we're not selling what we thought we would be selling. But often the advertising's fine. The marketing's fine. I mean, I had a client some years ago who spent two million dollars with me and I begged them to take a million of it off the table and use it to fix the sales component part of their business. I said, just pay me less. Let me do less. But pay me less and take this money and here's what you need to do with it. You need to fix the inbound call. You need to fix the salesperson who is actually face to face with this patient's life's client. Please, please, please, please, please. No, we want better ads. You don't need better ads if you have crappy salespeople, right? What's the point? You know, I mean, the football analogy is it's the red zone. So all the statistics in the middle of the field are nice. But if you can't get in the end zone and all you can do is kick field goals, you're still going to get beat, right? Right. Exactly. So Nick, one of the things that you talk about and you tell your team at GKIC and other people who you've trained is that everyone, every entrepreneur at their core needs to be a salesperson. Talk about that a little bit. Yeah, I couldn't agree. As the business owner, we talked about Dan brought up, it's your job to bring the money in. And many times that money is the sales, closing the sales for the organization. I think, you know, they get caught up in that or it's messy or they don't want to do that. And many of them haven't been trained or aren't good and they get in the way. We find that a lot also. And really everybody is a salesperson to the person answering the phone, to the waitress in the restaurant. She's a salesperson. We had an experience last night where she wasn't a good salesperson, right? A good salesperson will build a check average. We were actually afraid of her. We were afraid of her. We wanted to get out as fast as we could. But you know, a good well, they'll build the check average. They'll ask for the sale. They'll assume the sale. They'll bring the dessert to the table and ask people to pick. So really everybody is at the heart of it as a salesperson. Let's go to the extreme Dan brought up football, but I'll go to Hollywood, right? So if we turned the TV on right about now, we'd see the view. We'd see Ellen. We'd see all the show. Those are your favorite shows. Absolutely. Love Ellen. Love the dance sequence. That's actually what you're doing when you close the shade at the GKIC headquarters. See, I watched the view after we're being bid because of all the blood spurting But go ahead. No, no, no, I agree. So, you know, there's somebody from Hollywood selling their movie, right? They're selling. There's a musical person selling their album or their concert. There's an author selling his book. We're all selling. Movie, HBO movie, The Entourage. I love the part where he said they paid me two million dollars to make this movie. And his agent said, no, they paid you two million dollars to sell the movie. Your job is to go out there and sell the movie. And I think we're all... So I'll give you both ends of the extreme. So this morning I have a Panera bread with breakfast because we don't... They bring in lunch, but we don't... We don't eat. So I overhear two of the staff members talking. And so one says to the other, I worked at McDonald's before this. I'm making more money here. But at McDonald's, we were asked to upsell. Here we're expected to upsell. And I don't really like it. So, of course, she needs to be removed from the cash register and handed a broom. And she's only worth minimum wage, not $15 an hour, not $50 an hour. Nobody gets that, but that's the reality. And so at every level, you see to your point, you see people that not only don't know how to do it, don't understand it, they have disdain and distaste for it. It's like, that's dirty. Well, they've got to be removed from everywhere unless they are a person in a room who has no interaction whatsoever with the public. So if you've got, like in a dental practice, if you've got somebody who does the insurance billing, and as long as you don't let her answer the phone, leave the room in the back, see a patient even walking past them, that person's got to leave by the back door and not the front door. Then it doesn't matter if she hates sales. Right. But otherwise, you have big problems all the way to the top. So I thought about it about a month ago. So somebody asked me, it was a good question. Because I dabble as a stock market investor. Is there any commonality in your losses? Interesting. Good question. A really good question. So here's the commonality. Every single time I violate my own litmus test, which is that the CEO or a very top dog ought to have hardcore direct sales experience. They ought to have some time if sold belly to belly, nose to nose, toes to toes in the trenches, and they ought to still have enthusiasm for it. Every single time I've violated that, I've lost money. Wow. Wow. You know, why do you think it is so like, and we see this a lot with the professionals, with the doctors, with the lawyers, that they think somehow this is below them? Why do you think that is? And what is the fix for them? Hopefully it's watching this and taking notes and saying what we're going to do. But what is the psychology there? You know, I find it difficult because you've spent all this money to open up the practice, right? You've invested all this money to be the best at your profession, and you won't do the things you need to do to put the money in the pocket of the business owner, which is yourself. So I don't know why. And maybe if you let's... I do. Okay. I will let you get to that. And by the way, chains are fixing it. So the reason the thing like Aspen Dental exists is because the doctors don't want to do it. They put a salesperson in place to do that. Here's why though. Okay. And it comes down to a very fundamental like fact of life is everything involves a sale and some people sell and some people buy. So they were sold. Dr. Dennis, lawyer, etc. They were sold and sold hard that get this degree, spend eight years, get your white coat and your stethoscope and your certificate for the wall, and you won't have to do that. Sure. And so they bought that and they have bought it intellectually, emotionally. It's part of why they did what they did. It is deeply embedded. And so every time any of us tries to say, well, wait a minute, let's analyze your business. Let's see where the money comes from. Let's look at the top 5% in your field or in your community. That's so against what they already bought that breaking them free of its very difficult. Even in the face of facts. So if you look at our most successful members, so you let's talk about, for example, Dr. Charlie Martin, right, whatever a diamond members. Well, Dr. Martin, you know, his case size is humongous compared to what the average and he's a master salesman. When you see a dentist like in his case with say $40,000 cases, right, and a dentist with $4,000 cases, he may be 10 times better as a dentist than the $4,000 case value dentist. He might be doing dentistry that is 10 times more complicated than what that dentist is doing. Maybe, might, probably not. Okay, but what is certain is he's 10 times better as salesperson. Yeah, yeah, and he's making more money. It's that simple. If you go to the restaurant example, like you just mentioned, you know, restaurants that I mentioned all of the time, they should actually like caught me there is a linea in Chicago. And so talk about a really successful restaurant. You buy a ticket to go to the restaurant. It's like $250 for the ticket. And it's like a show. But then when you get there, they say, Oh, do you want the wine pairing? Well, yeah, I want the wine pairing. Well, we have a deluxe wine pairing. And so they're upselling there. And so really, it doesn't matter what your business is, does it? This is the function that brings in the money. Right? If it is not that, it is the place where a lot of money, differential money is left behind. So in certain times, for certain businesses, that's an affordable luxury in other times, including the ones in which we live now. And for many businesses, it proves to be an unaffordable luxury. And they, they starve their way to bankruptcy court based on the loss differential money. I think that's right. It's where the margin is, you know, it's really where the money goes into the pocket. And it brings them to the top 1% of the higher echelon. I think that 10 years ago, there was funny money floating around. Everybody could afford to leave it on the table. Now there isn't. I mean, that just you have to work too much harder. Our advertising has to work too harder. Our marketing has to work too much harder. The sales, the sales process has to be tight. It has to follow a system. So one of the things that we talk about is that sales is the fastest way, getting better at selling, training your people to get better at selling is the fastest way to increase your bottom line, because you don't have to spend any more money on marketing. Why don't you address that either of you? Well, to the point you just made. So every advertising and marketing step, none of it is getting less expensive. It is inflating at a greater, to greater heights faster than the inflation in price and in profits in almost every business. So the cost of getting somebody through the doors for the first time of any brick and mortar business, a restaurant and auto parts store, you name it, is going up by a wider margin at greater speed than is their internal inflation in price and margin. So if you draw those two trend lines on a chart, you eventually, and it, many businesses doesn't take long before the cost of getting people through the doors is so great in proportion to the sale that is being made that they cannot stay in business. And mid-range restaurants right now, perfect example, right? So Friday, Friday's announced last week they're selling all 276 of their restaurants to independent franchise owners and they're not going to be in the restaurant business. Really? It's because of this chart. It's because of this chart. Right. Right. If you do business online, same thing, the cost of getting qualified eyeballs to a site is going up by a greater ratio faster than is the inflation embedded in what it is that you sell. So in any business, the chart exists. We draw it a little different industry by industry, but it exists. And so the inevitable result of that chart regardless of what business you're in is depth unless you fix it. So you can fix it by somehow bringing those costs back down, which everybody works on a lot, right? Everybody works on that a lot. Catalog mailers are trying to figure out how to trim the page size to six by nine instead of eight by 10. And we can say, okay, so everybody works on this a lot, right? Or you can try and fix it by better monetizing the people you do get to walk through the doors. And that's not entirely sales based in retail, it's signage, traffic pattern, merchandising, et cetera. But it is largely sales based. And when you are creating leads and handing them to salespeople, this chart prohibits handing them to poor salespeople. Now it even prohibits handing them to mediocre salespeople. You can only afford to hand a good lead to a really good salesperson or a really good sales manager who then makes sure he's managing really good salespeople. And if you aren't working on that, all the work you do over here is band-aids over cancer. The chart's the same. It just takes longer to get for it to put you on the steps of bankruptcy court. I just want to remind everybody that we are going to be getting into step by step on how to solve all of these problems. But we have to lay the foundations first before we get into the actual techniques. So Nick, one of the things that you teach your guys and what you teach other salespeople is that there's a big difference between the consultative type of sale and the transactional sale. And by the way, thank you for the pen. And did you notice that, Dan? He took my pen along the way. I mean, you know, it's a price elasticity. What's the difference between the two pens? One sold for 500, the other one sold for 50 cents. And they both right. Quite frankly. You like yours better? Mine better. There you go. So what is the difference between that, the transactional sale and the consultative sale? Sure. The consultative sale was something that was coined in the 70s by a gentleman by the name of Mark Hanna. And really, it is a diagnostic interview. It's a step by step process. It's a series of questions that's meant to get to a benefit or an outcome. And it's a process that is put in place, and you really want to script it. You really want to have it move along the way because it allows the salesperson to be ever present in the conversation with the customer or the prospect. A transactional sale is really something that is like what we talked about the pen era. It's quick. It's easy. Usually the person is coming into the store. It may be price driven. But by having a system and a process, you could raise the average price. You could raise the average transaction level on both. And I'll use one example that could go both ways. So when I get back from this taping, I got to go get my son a suit for his homecoming dance. Because you are the fashion play. So you want him to be the fashion play to GKIC. I'm going to have to make sure he has a pocket square like Dan pointed out. But when you go there, these... This is for his prom. Homecoming. Homecoming. Pocket square up here. Condom down here. Absolutely. Absolutely. Just make sure you take care of that. Both ways. Yeah. I was wondering what something was going to happen. So, but they'll have a process, right? They'll have a process for the sale. They'll have the suit, the tie, he'll have two ties, socks, underwear. There's a process. When I was wearing suits more in the day to day, I got my suits made. He had a process also. Well, there's two different sales, but one is more consultive. What do you do? What are you doing? Are you sitting a lot? Are you in and out of cars? Are you in front of clients? What type of clients are you in front of? The other one is more transactional, but both has a system and both have a process and both have very high average tickets. In just a few minutes, we're going to be going over actually the six steps that you come up with in just a second. We're going to do that. So, one of the things that we have here is a bonus that comes with this program. It's something that we call scripts. There you go. Scripts. So, it's a bonus that comes with the program. A lot of salespeople, Dan, hate scripts. They want to wing it. They think it hurts their creativity and all of that stuff. Let's address that because if we don't solve this problem, then the next six steps are going to be real difficult. So, most dogs would prefer peeing and pooping anywhere they liked. That would be their natural instinctive preference. It's just, I feel a poop coming on. I take a poop. Nick often does that because when we domesticate this animal, we enforce a variety of rules about where they can poop and where they can be. So, we determine that, we train that, and we enforce that. So, salespeople are much the same. Left to their own devices, they would rather just poop and pee whenever they feel like it, wherever they feel like it, etc. However, if we wish to domesticate them and we wish to have them be productive, we can't let them do that. So, the fact that most salespeople prefer, they argue for spontaneity and they prefer not to script themselves, be scripted, be managed with scripts, be taught scripts. That tells us two things immediately. One, if most salespeople feel that way, we know it's wrong. So, even if we didn't know what was right, if we had never heard of scripts, we would know that was wrong. Because whatever most of them are doing is wrong because most of them fail. Most of them barely make a living, they wash out, they tend to move down the sales ladder. So, the guy who is selling fairly sophisticated products in the second year of his career is selling cars by the eighth year of his career. Why? Because he's a crappy salesperson. So, we would know it was wrong even if we didn't know it was right. Then, from a managerial standpoint, the dog analogy is valid. You wouldn't have to know what was right. If you walked into someone's house and there was dog poop and piss everywhere and the place smelled horrible and you had to be careful where you walked to get to the couch, if you had never had a dog, if you had never seen a dog before in your entire life, you just arrived from Mars, you would know this is not right. There is something wrong about this. Then, if you went and looked at top performers, so in any sales field, sophisticated, unsophisticated, to his point, there's a process for selling a cheap suit to a teenager and there's a process for selling. So, sophisticated, unsophisticated, B2B consumer domain average, you pick a category of selling and you find the hundred in the country who are earning the highest incomes in that category and you're going to find 98 of them are scripted. Now, most of them will have scripted themselves, but they are scripted. In stand-up comedy, there are a few, every generation, there's a few who literally go up there with no preconceived idea, their improv people, they play with the audience, Robin Williams who just died, capable, Jonathan Winters who he admired, capable, but we can name them in each generation because they are so few. And by the way, it's ever more dangerous to do that kind of comedy in today's political correctness environment. Everybody else has scripted pieces of shtick that they move in and out of. They have written them, every comedian is a writer, comedy writer, they write, Louis C. K. writes, Joan Rivers wrote, Seinfeld talks about that. Seinfeld is a writer, Larry David is a writer, these people write their stuff, then they memorize it, then they go do it somewhere that doesn't do any harm until they get exactly the way they want and then they lock it in and they do it that way forever more. That's how top salespeople perform. And further, sales trainers who have longevity in that career are all script guys, Tommy Hopkins, before him J. Douglas Edwards, Sandler, these were or are, depending on whether they're living or dead, all script guys, the sales trainers who come and go, who are the flavor and fad of a year or two in corporate America, usually hired by people who've never sold, don't know how to sell. So they don't know good from bad. These guys are not, and many of them pander to the desire of the majority of the salespeople by selling, non-scripted selling. But their careers and sales trainers are very short-lived because it doesn't work. Right, absolutely. We mentioned Jerry Seinfeld that there's a great movie called Comedian. If you haven't seen it, have you seen Comedian? It's a documentary and it shows just how hard he works on his craft, how he did exactly what you said. This is Seinfeld after he's made a gazillion dollars working in open mic nights to practice his material with the notes that he wrote, the script that he wrote. Lock out with the handwritten notes, put it on the stool and keep on going. One of the things, we were talking about Joan Rivers earlier, I was impressed by when you saw her being interviewed before a show, she was writing out them on big, big boards that she would put on the floor. Yeah, she's got to take them to the... People would take them and want to decide, she's like, you know, that's my work, those are my words. But also in the interview, when I was watching a documentary about her, they walked through her house and she was an avid reader. I mean, she had bookshelves in every room and she was a student of the word and a student of the script. So there's a saying, writers write what they read. If you aren't... If your intake is not voluminous and it is not both broad and deep, so depth is... So if you are a high-end clothing salesperson or you write copy, you need a depth of historical and current knowledge about men's fashion. But you also need much broader, you know, eclectic interests. But yeah, you won't find... So you will not find a great marketer who is not a great student, you won't find a great comedian who's not a great student, you won't find a great salesperson who's not a great student. We were just at Titans of Direct Marketing, put on by Boardroom, and Marty Edelston, who built Boardroom from the ground up. And Boardroom has probably sold more $50 books by mail than any company past or present. Brian Kirchman Boardroom is speaking at Infosummit 2014 and one of the things he's showing is the whole case history of their diabetes book campaign. Marty was, you know, one of his six pillars of life was insatiable curiosity. I mean, if in his presence anybody mentioned a book, he at least got it and looked at it. Charlie... I saw Charlie Munger, Warren Buffett's partner, recently interviewed. And Charlie, he reads everything he can get his hands on about biology. And he has made a correlation from biology to choosing investments, not just in the health care, but in general. And so, yeah, the salesperson who... All of the things that you see bad sales people do, right? They refuse to work from scripts, they refuse to prepare, they're not real good students. They're... As soon as the day is done, they're at the strip club, you know, they're not at the library. I mean, there's a reason, 80% of them hardly make a living. And these are all the reasons. You know, I think the interesting thing is also that they're all curious. The greatest salespeople I've ever... They are curious. They're human... They're students of the human behavior and they want to know what makes people tick. Well, and see, a diagnostic cell, as you described, is... It is scripted, but it works best if it is in the hands of a person intelligent enough about a lot of things to be able to move off that script a little bit, coming back to it. Any person who is intelligent enough to be intellectually curious to actually want to know, not just ask a question and then wait to ask question number four on the list, sort of, not mattering what Dave says has his answer, but to be actually curious about what this person does, what makes them tick, what their attitudes are. So, you know, again, as a bonus that you get with this system, Nick, you've put in here some really tremendous scripts that you've written for your guys, everything from upselling to thank you calls. So, everything, I guess, is... You've scripted everything with your team. We tried to script everything. It goes back to what Dan says, you know, you hang around with animals long enough to know if you realize you need to tame them. And so, you need to... That was a much more elegant way of saying it, by the way. But I think you have to script it. And I think... Because what we've talked about, the pros will make it their own, right? They'll get to the core and the nuances of it, but they will... They'll put their own words in, their own language in, their own voice inflection, but it really needs to be scripted. We wouldn't go see a movie when everybody's just talking. Larry David was the only one to do that successfully on HBO other than that. You really want everything scripted. You want them to own the script and we really take a lot of time and we tweak it because every morning we have a stand up and we have some programs to talk a lot about that and teach people how to do that. But we really say, what are we hearing? You know, and we... What's working in the scripts? What do we need to tweak? What needs to be motivated? And sometimes you'll go down a path and the script will be bringing you one thing, but you need to make a quick adjustment because there's something more pressing. And so, you want to get that to the front of the script and move it along. Fantastic. And again, they're right in here, so you just got to check that out. Make sure when you get your program that you check that out. Okay, because those are scripts that you can... That they can model. Sure. All right. So, we're finally going to those are some of the foundations. Let's go to the NoBS Six Step System, Dan. And we're going to go into great depth on each one of these steps except for the last step during our time in this program. But we'll talk about how we're going to get to the last step. Let's just go over each one of them briefly and then we'll go deep into them later on. The first thing is permission to sell. What is permission to sell? So, you can sell without permission. It's sort of in the rape category and it has... It usually has negative consequences just like that does. Um, principally, if not arrest and imprisonment, banishment. So, if you are selling by true force without permission, without the willing cooperation and participation of the buyer, you will... If you do it in a community of any kind where there is word of mouth, you will soon be banished. Even by people who are satisfied with what they bought because the process is so ugly that they will talk about the process, not about their happiness and satisfaction with what they bought. And you will certainly... Even if you're not banished from a community, you will sort of be prohibited from any after the transaction value in that relationship. Somebody may buy that way but they're certainly not going to refer anybody to you and put them through that same process. They're really not going to want to buy anything else from you again even if what they bought was just fine. So, it is better to have a cooperating and willing participant and that starts with permission to start selling. So, that's as simple as, you know, Nick, do you have time for this conversation now? Or if not, let's just... Let's just book a time to get together for lunch next week. Okay, that's as simple as, you know, to more complicated, but permission to start selling at the very start of the selling, to permission for almost every sort of benchmark along the way. Right? So, so my tailor... So, the stage stuff I have custom made, the other stuff I don't, but because I don't care, but the stage stuff I do. So, he's not the sharpest pencil in the box, but he does have a sensitivity to how to handle each customer. So, he knows better now than to sort of surprise me when I come in because the tailor shop and the dry cleaners are owned by the same guy in this small town, which is very smart. Okay? Because he gets me out of there every month or more often with cleaning, but he's learned not to try and move me six steps to look at the new stuff when I'm in there for that because I'm doing it five minutes before I'm meeting somebody for dinner, you know, and so he now calls. Right? So, you're coming in to pick up cleaning. We got cleaning here for you. When are you coming in? Okay. Could you allow an extra 15 minutes because the new fabric swatches are in and there's a couple things I know you're going to... Okay. So, that's permission to sell. Right? And now, he's got permission to start this process, that he's got a whole bunch of benchmarks. He's got to get through all the way to you trying something on. Right? So, permission to sell is something clumsy, awkward salespeople don't understand. It's also where marketing fits sort of seamlessly because marketing leading up to the selling can get the permission in advance for that person to do his thing. So, he doesn't have to be particularly sophisticated and adept at it. The marketing has done it for him. Got it. Got it. So, permission to sell is number one. Step two is the offer. Now, Nick, we make a lot of offers at GKIC. Your sales folks are calling up people with offers all the time. Just discuss that a little bit and again, we're going to get into more depth later on. Well, you know, I think what it has to be, it has to be clear and it has to be concise. You know, at the end of the day, people enjoy to buy, but they have to be told how to buy and that's really where the offer comes into place. A confused mind won't buy. So, you put everything on the table in front of somebody, you know, a catalog, so to speak, and say, you know, which one you want or throwing the marbles on the table, so to speak, no one is really going to be purchased. So, it needs to be clear and concise. Okay. There's more than one place for the offer, by the way. So, again, to his consultative sale model, the diagnostic call, whatever it is called, or the diagnostic appointment in healthcare, the actual exam and diagnosis, that's an offer. And it should be structured like an offer. So, like, we have things for diagnostic processes of the seven things you will know, the seven questions that will be answered by this diagnostic call. So, there's a benefit part of that offer. The onset is, even if we don't go any further and you don't want to do business with me, as a result of the call alone, you're getting some value because you will know the answer to these questions. So, there's that offer all the way to the offer of, here, it's six cups in a box and you can have your logo on them and you get saucers for free if you do it by 10 o'clock tomorrow morning. Right. Got it. We're going to just skip over the next one, which is presentation, because we're going to discuss presentation. Dan, emotional logic, talk about that, because that's a really cool one, which I don't think a lot of people are aware of. It is to the best of my knowledge, a zig-ziggler lingo. And it refers to the blending of fact-figure statistics, rational reasons to buy, which most marketers, copywriters, and sales professionals put too much weight on or get to too soon. So, they are telling you facts about single stitching versus double stitching and before you even have decided you want to search. So, there's either too much weight put on it or it gets too quickly. And some people rely too much on emotion and don't have then any rational basis for which a purchase can be made. And there's a place in the middle of that where it blends and zig called it emotional logic. Yeah, beautiful. Closing the sale, we're going to cover closing the sale in tremendous depth. But after we close the sale, the fifth piece to the puzzle, actually the sixth piece to the puzzle, is the morning after. What does that mean? Well, I think it's, you know, it's the, it's the, you bought what conversation, you know, and it's, it's, it's, you got that in there actually. Right, right. So, it's the, you did what, what happened, what did you buy? And it allows the person to sell his support network or sell people on, in his peer group. It allows him to justify the sale. Plus, it allows us, it goes back to the money, you know, second money is easier to get than first money. And so, it allows us to go up the pedestal and get more and more of their dollars. So, we're not going to be discussing that during this, during the DVD portion of this. What we're going to do is, we're going to do a bonus call later on, which we'll tell folks about. We're going to be doing a bonus call where we're discussing actual strategies that how to eliminate buyers remorse is really what that is. Dan, anything to add to that? Well, so it's actually three things. So, it is the elimination of buyers remorse. So, in some environments, that's about actual refund. Okay. Oh, I, I came to an online launch and I made a purchase and I wake up tomorrow morning and I work the hell. So, or I went to a, I went to a physical room. And even if I didn't spend money, I booked, I went to a dog and pony show and I booked an appointment for three days from now for, to come in for a diagnostic process with a financial advisor and I wake up the next morning and I don't want to do that. Right? It seemed like a good idea in the room. But now that I think about, I got this questionnaire, I got to gather up all these records. I got to go in there and strip naked. I got out. I don't want to do that. So, it is about buyers remorse. Right? It is to his point about second money. We'll use his term. Second money, which done right is easier to get than the first money. Right? So, it is about second money. And the third thing it's about is future customer value and or referral value because that really gets put in place has a possibility or not a possibility the morning after. So, it is really about all three of those things. Fantastic. And again, I think the really important point here, which is we're going to get to some questions in just a second, but a really important point is there's sales all along the way. So, I don't, I'm not sure that the financial advisor actually thinks of, well, I'm actually selling the appointment. And I've got to do everything I can to keep that sale before I go to the next sale in the sales process. Everything is selling. When the person, the note I took here is a front desk person who answers the call, their greets the person when they come in, they're selling, aren't they? I mean, everything has got to be scripted. All of this has to be scripted. In the script book here that you have, Nick, you've got, you've got that for when somebody bought, for example, a launch product for that when somebody becomes a new member. And those are all different scripts and they all have a specific outcome. You know, at the end of the day, sometimes sales people have a tendency to rush through the process. And this really, you know, if you have a system, it allows them to kind of take a conscious step by step through the process. But really, the sixth step is the most, one of the most important things too, because no one wants refunds, no one really wants to get the ball over the goal line than to see the flag come and have it pulled back. And, you know, then they're head under the hood and they're looking at everything. So I think, you know, it is the key and the pros really know this and really know how to romance the morning after, so to speak, and make it just as much fun as the evening before. So we're going to go through each one of these steps. And again, the sixth step, we're going to do a special bonus call about, okay, so we've got some questions here from folks who are watching us live. And what is that? What are you doing, there we go. I was just going to make him up. We do have people watching us. Is that Lonzi? Is that the name? Lonzi? Lonzi. That's sort of a Chicago Al Capone era, these and those. Yeah, low level gangster name. Well, my peer group is called Lonzi. I haven't got to Lonzi yet. You went past Lonzi. I got to get, we just insulted Lonzi. At least we can answer your question. Okay, so what about preparing the structure of the presentation rather than scripting it entirely? Again, okay, so he asks the question because that's what he would prefer. To do, right? And most people would prefer to do it that way. But see, language matters. Words matter. Say, if people are paying attention, he's hypersensitive to language. He's doing this on the fly, but he's trained and conditioned himself to be hypersensitive to language. Copywriters, good ones, are hypersensitive to language. So if for a 16 page sales ladder, which will take roughly the average person 30, 48 minutes to read, you would agonize for an hour over a choice of a word or a sentence. Why wouldn't you put equal angst into getting it right in a 48 minute face to face sales presentation, right? In some respects, the stakes are higher. Because direct mail, if you get 2% conversion, you're a hero. If you're selling in the Cadillac dealership and you only convert 2% of the people you meet with, you aren't going to be in the Cadillac dealership very long, right? So why wouldn't you? Some pros, real pros. The correct thing to do is to write it word for word until you have it right. Then if you want to back out of that to a structural outline that is all you use to refresh yourself, fine. But just to do the outline, no way. It's better than nothing, but it is not real professional selling. It's just not. Yeah, and so I do a lot of stage presentations as do you, Dan. And if I'm going to script anything word for word, it's going to absolutely be my opening with the first things that are coming out of my mouth. The thing I'm going to script the least is the actual teaching portion of it. But then my close, oh baby, that thing is scripted word for word. Also, again, the question, I think people think that if it's not scripted, I have more freedom. I think it's the opposite. I think it's the opposite. Well, it depends on how you define freedom. Well, I mean being able to really make the sale in a, well, and focus on my prospect. Exactly. It gives you greater freedom to focus on the other person or persons, right? And feedback, body or spoken, all of that because you have a solid foundation underneath you that you've already learned, preferably memorized, rehearsed, or familiar with. So you have no part of you has to be thinking about what I'm going to say, right? It does, of course, restrain you to a path. So if you want the freedom to just roam, if you define freedom that way, right? So again, what I often do when I'm brought into a copy for a company that has salespeople, humans at the end of the chain, is I will record them and get that transcribed. And it's usually horrifying, right? Absolutely. It is usually horrifying for me, because I know I am going to have some accountability for a result that I am delivering prospects into the hands of these roaming free creatures. It's horrifying for the client, because I immediately go and say, right? And very often the same person, three presentations in a row, you wouldn't know, not only when you know it was the same person, you would know it was the same product or the same company. Now, there has to be of six stories that make point A. There's got to be one that's more effective than the other. There has to be. Why wouldn't we want to use the most effective one every single time? Right. So it's a false freedom to be left alone to roam. Yeah, absolutely. And again, one of the bonuses that you got to keep referring to is, because I want, you know, is Nick's script book that he put together. So again, you want to go through this and structure these scripts work. So these aren't just things that are tested. So go through and model them for your own scripts. Okay, so in this segment, we've gone over the foundations of sales success. And we actually went over the six steps in the No BS sales process. In the next session, we're going to be talking about how you develop and how you create a really powerful, irresistible offer. So that's coming up in part two, and you absolutely, positively don't want to miss it.
